text: Namaste, my name is Dr. Vishal Soni and today we will be watching a case of endoscopic TEP, Reeves-Dropa repair for umbilical hernia with divarication of recti done robotically. I give you greetings from the Zydus Hospitals Ahmedabad and this is an introduction to LARCH, HAWKS and HERNIAX. These are our MIS courses. More details in the description. So the first part of the surgery actually looks a little shabby and shoddy. However, this is an essential step. We put in the first spot in the left subcostal region and I do something called as a scope dissection. I create the initial space using the camera of the robot itself. One can do it laparoscopically when doing a laparoscopic surgery. I put in the first port under vision and use an instrument that you can see that is a bipolar cutter and create some more space so that two more ports which are robotic in nature can be placed inside. So anatomically speaking, on the floor you have the posterior rectus sheath and on the roof one can appreciate the rectus abdominis. The medial edge is the junction of the ARS, PRS and laterally we have the linea seminalis and the retromuscular space is the space in the center that one can appreciate. Of course, one can complete the entire dissection of the left RMS using one of the handheld instruments. However, at this stage, I decide to proceed with my robotic foot placements. So I take help of these spinal needles, they are G needles and they give me the direction and the depth using which I would be placing my robotic port. One needs to make sure that they don't injure the left sided inferior epigastric otherwise it's a nightmare to begin with.

The surgeon clears the attachment between the posterior rectus sheath and the anterior rectus sheath using robotic instruments within a confined space of approximately seven centimeters. The junction of these sheaths is visible on the screen. The surgeon carefully divides the posterior rectus sheath about three millimeters below the junction, ensuring the peritoneum remains intact to prevent compromising the surgical space. The dissection continues in the left retromuscular space, with precise movements to avoid entering the cavity.

The surgeon demonstrates the cut edge of the posterior rectus sheath, with the peritoneum visible beyond it, abutting the thinned-out linea alba due to divarication. A small layer of the linea alba is preserved on the peritoneum to prevent complications from its thinness. The robotic platform provides stable vision and tremor-free movements, aiding in precise dissection and peeling off the peritoneum from the divarication area.

The surgeon shifts focus cranially, using backward movements to divide the posterior rectus sheath and any intervening tissue between incisions. The surgical field is tight, with the thinned-out peritoneum visible on the floor as a translucent membrane, and the divarication of the recti on the roof. The central area of the screen shows the hernia, with circumferential dissection being performed around it, avoiding direct manipulation of the hernia itself due to the risk of tearing the thinned-out peritoneum and hernia sac.

The surgeon focuses on the posterior rectus sheath on the right side, identifying it by the orientation of the fibers. The loose areolar tissue of the right-sided retro muscular space is visible, resembling a club sandwich as the surgeon dissects between the layers. The dissection continues cranially and caudally to match the left side, ensuring the inter-rectus distance is approximately two to three centimeters.

The surgeon continues dissection, focusing on the left-sided posterior rectus sheath at the cranial end, after creating a large space on the right side. The preperitoneal space in the center and the retro muscular spaces on both sides are connected, forming a common large space for the placement of prostheses to reinforce the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon focuses on the last portion of tissue hanging from the roof, identified as the hernia. The peritoneum is carefully dissected and peeled away from the skin and subcutaneous tissue to prevent any holes in the skin. The peritoneum is thinned out and divided, ensuring a tension-free closure of the defect. The posterior elements are successfully positioned on the floor, marking the end of the dissection.

The surgeon measures the space with a scale and places a large prosthesis to cover the hernia and repair the divarication of recti. The infraumbilical area is cleared of fibro fatty tissue, revealing the right-sided inferior epigastric vessels. Dissection is performed laterally, preserving the neurovascular bundles visible from the floor to the roof. The umbilical hernia is exposed, and the divarication is stripped of peritoneum. The surgeon plans closure of the herniated defect using barbed sutures and demonstrates a camera inversion technique, making the anterior divarication appear as if suturing on the floor.

The surgeon utilizes the robotic platform to perform suturing on the abdominal wall, taking advantage of camera inversion and hand switch techniques for infraumbilical suturing. A small bite is taken from the subcutaneous tissue to reduce seroma formation. Conventional suturing is performed for the supraumbilical component, with a horizontal mattress technique used near the port for better ergonomics and strength. The suturing progresses towards the umbilical defect with slight overlap to ensure a tight repair.

The surgeon closes the defect in the posterior elements, including the posterior rectus sheath (PRS) and peritoneum. A scale is used to measure the dissected cavity, and a tailored mesh, either 20x15 or 25x25, is trimmed and placed flat in the cavity. The mesh placement is checked by deflating and re-inflating the cavity to ensure it sits well, marking the end of the surgery.
